output_text:
Cleaned up the introduction by tightening prose, removing contradictions (the "novel attention mechanism" claim vs. the off-the-shelf GAT), aligning the stated contributions with what the paper actually does, and cutting filler.

---

We present GraphClarifyKG, a knowledge-graph-grounded ranker for clarifying questions in conversational search. Existing systems treat clarifying-question selection as flat text ranking and discard the entity structure of the underlying KG. We instead condition the ranker on a retrieved entity subgraph, combining a standard cross-encoder over (query, candidate) with a graph encoder over the entities mentioned in the query.

**Setup.** Given a user query *q* and a candidate clarifying question *c*, prior systems score (*q*, *c*) with a cross-encoder. KG-grounded variants additionally extract entities from *q*, retrieve their *k*-hop neighborhoods from Wikidata, and encode the resulting subgraph with a Graph Attention Network. We follow this pipeline.

**Method.** Entities are initialized with pretrained TransE embeddings and propagated through a 2-layer GAT using the hyperparameters of Veličković et al. (2018). The mean-pooled entity embedding is concatenated with the cross-encoder representation of (*q*, *c*) and passed through an MLP scoring head trained with a margin loss. Both components are off-the-shelf, and the full system trains end-to-end in under three GPU-hours on a single A100.

**Results.** On ClariQ-KG — a new benchmark we release with this paper, pairing CAsT-style clarifying-question pools with Wikidata entity links — GraphClarifyKG reaches Recall@1 of 0.61, versus 0.54 for a cross-encoder baseline without graph context.

**Contributions.** (i) ClariQ-KG, the first dataset linking CAsT-style clarifying-question pools to Wikidata entities; (ii) cross-encoder and KG-augmented baselines that establish task difficulty and a reference point for future work on graph-grounded clarification.

---

**Edits worth flagging:**
- Removed the "central contribution is a novel graph attention mechanism" sentence. The Method section describes a stock 2-layer GAT with default hyperparameters from Veličković et al. — that is not a novel mechanism, and reviewers will catch the mismatch. The real contributions, per the summary, are the dataset and the baselines; I aligned the framing accordingly.
- Dropped "yielding a question-specific subgraph embedding tailored to the conversational state" — the method uses mean-pooled entity embeddings with no conversational-state conditioning described, so this overclaims.
- Cut "We attribute the gain to the inclusion of entity-neighborhood information." A 7-point Recall@1 gap with no ablation does not support attribution. If you have an ablation, cite it; if not, drop the claim or move it to a hedged discussion.
- Folded the dataset announcement into Results so the contributions list is not the first place the reader hears about it.

**Things to verify before submission:**
- That the GAT hyperparameters genuinely match Veličković et al. (2018) — if you tuned heads, hidden dim, or dropout, say so.
- The 0.61 vs. 0.54 comparison: confirm the baseline is trained on the same data with matched compute, otherwise the delta is not clean.
- Whether "first dataset pairing CAsT-style clarifying-question pools with Wikidata entity links" is actually first — ClariQ and several follow-ups have entity-linked variants; a quick check against Aliannejadi et al. and the TREC CAsT 2022 release is worth doing.
